title: ConfigMaps and Secrets
learningObjectives: Create and use ConfigMaps for application configuration
Use Secrets for sensitive data with proper security practices
Mount ConfigMaps and Secrets as volumes or environment variables
Understand Secret types and encryption at rest

theory.content:
ConfigMaps

ConfigMaps store non-sensitive configuration data as key-value pairs. They decouple configuration from container images.

apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
data:
  LOG_LEVEL: "info"
  DATABASE_URL: "postgres://db:5432/myapp"
  config.yaml: |
    server:
      port: 8080
      timeout: 30s

Using ConfigMaps

# As environment variables
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: app-config
      env:
        - name: LOG_LEVEL
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: app-config
              key: LOG_LEVEL

# As a volume (files)
      volumeMounts:
        - name: config-volume
          mountPath: /etc/config
  volumes:
    - name: config-volume
      configMap:
        name: app-config

Secrets

Secrets store sensitive data (passwords, tokens, keys). Data is base64-encoded — NOT encrypted by default. Enable encryption at rest in the API server for real security.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-credentials
type: Opaque
stringData:              # Plain text (auto-encoded)
  username: admin
  password: "S3cr3tP@ss"

Secret Types

  • Opaque: Generic key-value pairs
  • kubernetes.io/tls: TLS certificates (tls.crt, tls.key)
  • kubernetes.io/service-account-token: Service account tokens
  • kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson: Docker registry credentials

Best Practices

  • Enable encryption at rest in etcd
  • Use external secret managers (AWS Secrets Manager, Vault) with Kubernetes External Secrets Operator
  • Set RBAC to restrict Secret access
  • Never commit Secret values to Git
